constraints: Core AGECO requirements (18 credits): AGECO103, AGECO220, AGECO256, AGECO301, AGECO315, AGECO330.
Advanced AGECO requirements (9 credits): AGECO351, AGECO455, AGECO492.
Three AGECO electives (9 credits) chosen from 300/400-level approved list; at least two at the 300-level or above.
Mathematics and statistics: MATH115 and STAT211.
Minimum grade of C required in all major courses.
Additional general education, social science, and humanity requirements apply per SEBS guidelines.
notes: Prepares students for careers in agribusiness, farm management, food industry, and agricultural finance.
Internship strongly recommended; AGECO480 (Agricultural Entrepreneurship) satisfies entrepreneurship elective.

constraints: Core ANSC sequence: ANSC101, ANSC200, ANSC201, ANSC210, ANSC220.
Advanced ANSC core: ANSC301, ANSC311, ANSC370, ANSC492.
Four ANSC electives (12 credits) from approved list; at least three at the 300-level or above.
Chemistry sequence: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308.
Biology sequence: BIO115, BIO117, BIO116, BIO118.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Concentrations available: Companion Animals, Equine Science, Livestock Production, Pre-Veterinary.
Pre-veterinary students should complete additional upper-level biology and chemistry courses.

constraints: Biology sequence: BIO115, BIO117, BIO116, BIO118.
Chemistry sequence: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308, CHEM309, CHEM310.
Three advanced electives (9 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved MCB/CHEM list.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: This BS is jointly offered by SEBS and SAS; students may be enrolled through either school.
Strong preparation for graduate study, medical school, pharmacy school, or biotechnology careers.
Research experience (MCB/CHEM independent research credits) strongly encouraged.

constraints: Joint program administered by SEBS and the School of Engineering.
Engineering foundation (first two years): same as SOE core including ENG101/102, CHEM159/160, MATH151/152/244/251, PHYS123/124/227/229, ISE343.
Environmental Sciences core: ENVSCI201, ENVSCI202, ENVSCI303.
BSYSE core (18 credits): BSYSE200, BSYSE210, BSYSE220, BSYSE230, BSYSE301.
Advanced BSYSE (9 credits): BSYSE310, BSYSE320, BSYSE400.
Three technical electives from approved BSYSE/ENVSCI/CEE list.
Minimum grade of C in all engineering and science courses.
notes: Accredited by ABET through the School of Engineering.
Focuses on application of engineering principles to biological, agricultural, and environmental systems.
Combines elements of Civil, Environmental, and Biological engineering.

constraints: Core sequence: BIOTECH210, BIOTECH215, BIOTECH301, BIOTECH311.
Chemistry sequence: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308.
Biology sequence: BIO115, BIO117, BIO116, BIO118.
Four electives (12 credits) from approved list; at least three at the 300-level or above.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Interdisciplinary program spanning agricultural, industrial, and medical biotechnology.
Students interested in regulatory careers should take BIOTECH420.
Research thesis option available for students with 3.0+ GPA.

constraints: Interdisciplinary program drawing on environmental, atmospheric, and social sciences.
Science core: ENVSCI101, ENVSCI201, ENVSCI202, ENVSCI303, BIO115, BIO117, CHEM161, CHEM162.
Atmospheric science: METEOR101, METEOR121, METEOR431, METEOR450.
Climate and society bridge: ENVSCI430, HUMEC410.
Four electives (12 credits) from approved interdisciplinary list; at least three at 300-level or above.
Writing: EXPOS101. Mathematics: MATH151. Statistics: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Program prepares students for careers in climate policy, environmental consulting, sustainability, and research.
Students are encouraged to complete a minor in Economics, Geography, or a related field.
Research or internship experience strongly recommended.

constraints: Introduction and core: EENR101, EENR201, EENR202, EENR220.
Five electives (15 credits) from approved EENR/ENVSCI/related list; at least four at 300-level or above.
Science foundation: BIO115, BIO117, BIO116, BIO118, CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171.
Field experience strongly encouraged; EENR305 fulfills field methods requirement.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Concentrations available: Wildlife Ecology, Conservation Biology, Forest Resources, Wetland and Aquatic Ecology.
GIS skills (ENVSCI385) strongly recommended for all concentrations.
Many students pursue careers in wildlife management, conservation, environmental consulting, or graduate study.

constraints: Interdisciplinary program combining environmental science with economics and business.
Economics core: ECON201, ECON202, AGECO103, AGECO220.
Environmental economics: AGECO351, AGECO330, AGECO400, AGECO420.
Four electives (12 credits) from approved AGECO/ECON/ENVSCI list; at least three at 300-level.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Ideal for students interested in environmental policy, natural resource economics, sustainability consulting, or public policy.
Students are encouraged to minor in Public Policy or Statistics.

constraints: Interdisciplinary program in the Department of Human Ecology.
Core environmental: ENVSCI101, ENVSCI201, ENVSCI322, EENR101, EENR410.
Human ecology core: HUMEC101, HUMEC310, HUMEC401, HUMEC410, HUMEC415, HUMEC420.
Four electives (12 credits) from approved interdisciplinary list; at least three at 300-level.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Program focuses on the human dimensions of environmental change and the role of institutions and behavior in sustainability.
Strong preparation for law school, public policy graduate programs, government, and NGO careers.
Internship with an environmental organization or government agency strongly recommended.

constraints: Four electives (12 credits) from approved interdisciplinary science list; at least three at 300-level.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Concentrations available: Environmental Chemistry, Environmental Biology, Environmental Geology (joint with Earth and Planetary Sciences), and Soil and Water Sciences.
GIS skills (ENVSCI385) are required and valuable for all concentrations.
Students are encouraged to pursue undergraduate research opportunities.

constraints: Microbiology prerequisite: MICROB316 and MICROB317 required before FS205.
Food Science core (27 credits): FS203, FS204, FS205, FS206, FS215, FS301, FS302, FS303, FS370.
Advanced Food Science (9 credits): FS380, FS404, FS492.
Chemistry: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308.
Two electives (6 credits) from approved FS/NUTRSCI/MICROB list at 300-level or above.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
IFT (Institute of Food Technologists) approved program.
notes: Program is accredited/approved by the Institute of Food Technologists (IFT).
Internship in the food industry strongly recommended and available for credit (FS491).
Students interested in regulatory careers should take FS405.

constraints: Human Ecology core: HUMEC101, HUMEC201, HUMEC231, HUMEC240.
Applied core: HUMEC310, HUMEC320, HUMEC340, HUMEC401.
Four electives (12 credits) from approved HUMEC/ENVSCI/EENR list; at least three at 300-level.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Interdisciplinary program examining the relationship between humans and their natural and built environments.
Prepares students for careers in social services, community development, environmental education, and public policy.
Students may pursue a concentration in Environmental Education or Family and Consumer Science.

constraints: Professional degree (BSLA); accredited by LAAB (Landscape Architectural Accreditation Board).
Studio sequence (core): LA102, LA201, LA301, LA401, LA490, LA491.
Technical requirements: LA202, LA302 (site engineering), LA205 (graphics).
History and theory: LA204, LA303, LA304.
Urban and ecological design: LA310, LA402, LA410, LA420.
Thesis: LA490 and LA491 (6 credits total).
All LA studio courses require a grade of C or better to advance.
Students must maintain satisfactory progress in the studio sequence.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
notes: The BSLA is a professional degree qualifying graduates to sit for the Landscape Architect Registration Examination (LARE).
Internship (professional practice experience) strongly encouraged and may be required for licensure.
Students with a prior bachelor's degree may be eligible for the MLA (Master of Landscape Architecture) accelerated path.

constraints: Marine core: MARINE100, MARINE200, MARINE210, MARINE220.
Science foundation: BIO115, BIO117, BIO116, BIO118, CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM307.
Mathematics: MATH151, MATH152. Physics: PHYS123, PHYS124.
Three electives (9 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved list.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Field trips to marine research stations (e.g., Rutgers Marine Field Station, Tuckerton) are integral to the program.
Prepares students for graduate study in oceanography, marine biology, coastal management, or marine policy careers.
MARINE360 (Marine Field Methods) involves mandatory weekend field trips.

constraints: Three electives (9 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved METEOR/ENVSCI list.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: AMS (American Meteorological Society) course certification standards are incorporated.
Students are encouraged to complete a minor in Mathematics, Computer Science, or Environmental Sciences.
The Rutgers Weather Lab provides operational forecasting experience.
METEOR421 (Numerical Weather Prediction) requires programming background (CS101 or equivalent recommended).

constraints: Chemistry: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308.
Three electives (9 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved MICROB/MCB list.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Strong preparation for graduate school in microbiology, public health, medicine, or biotechnology.
Students interested in medical or clinical careers should include MICROB410 (Medical Microbiology).
Research credit (independent study) available for 3.0+ GPA students.

constraints: Chemistry: CHEM161, CHEM162, CHEM171, CHEM163, CHEM307, CHEM308.
Two electives (6 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved list.
Writing: EXPOS101. Math: MATH115. Stats: STAT211.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Prepares students for dietetic internship programs (DI) leading to Registered Dietitian Nutritionist (RDN) credential.
Students planning to become RDNs should consult with the program director for ACEND-approved didactic program requirements.
Research opportunities available through Rutgers Center for Lipid Research and other labs.

constraints: Advanced core: PBIO301, PBIO310, PBIO320, PBIO330.
Three electives (9 credits) at the 300-level or above from approved PBIO/EENR/MCB list.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Prepares students for graduate study in botany, plant biology, plant genomics, or careers in horticulture and agriculture.
Students interested in biotechnology should take PBIO410 and BIOTECH301.
The greenhouse facilities and botanical gardens provide hands-on research opportunities.

constraints: Four electives (12 credits) from approved PLSC/PBIO/related list; at least three at 300-level.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: Concentrations available: Agronomy, Horticulture, Plant Protection (Pest Management), and Sustainable Agriculture.
Students in the Plant Protection concentration should emphasize PLSC230, PLSC300, PLSC310, PLSC320.
Prepares students for careers in crop production, extension services, plant breeding, and sustainable agriculture.

constraints: Plant Science support: PLSC200, PLSC220, PLSC320.
Turfgrass core: TURF100, TURF200, TURF210, TURF300.
Three electives (9 credits) from approved TURF/PLSC/AGECO list; at least two at 300-level.
Minimum grade of C in all major courses.
notes: One of the oldest and most distinguished turfgrass programs in the United States.
Students gain hands-on experience at the Rutgers Horticultural Farm and golf course facilities.
Graduates work in golf course management, sports turf, lawn care, sod production, and landscape management.
Students interested in the business side should add AGECO electives.
